history: The patient has developed a strong sense of identity and belonging within their research lab, finding camaraderie and accomplishment there. The departure of a close coworker was emotionally significant and may have triggered feelings of loss and anxiety about changes in the lab's social environment. Their personal life is described as less predictable and comfortable, suggesting a preference for the structure and support found in the lab. These experiences likely shaped their beliefs about safety, control, and connection.
intermediate_belief: If I am not fully prepared or do not perform well in important lab situations, others will see me as incompetent or unreliable, and I will lose the sense of belonging that makes me feel secure. I must always be in control and ready to avoid negative outcomes. If I am not prepared, I risk being exposed or letting others down.
intermediate_belief_depression: If I can't keep up with the demands or changes in the lab, it means I'm not good enough and I don't really belong anywhere. No matter how much I try, I might still end up alone or failing.
coping_strategies: The patient tends to engage in meticulous planning and over-preparation for lab-related tasks to manage anxiety and maintain a sense of control. When overwhelmed, they may procrastinate or avoid tasks, and may seek reassurance or withdraw socially after experiencing loss or change in their environment.
situation: Preparing for an important discussion scheduled in January. Preparing for a lab meeting involving troubleshooting equipment and learning programming.
auto_thought: If I don't get everything right in this meeting, everyone will see that I can't handle it. I might lose my place in the group.
behavior: I spend extra time double-checking my work and rehearsing what I will say. I avoid asking for help because I don't want to look unprepared. Sometimes I put off starting the preparation because it feels overwhelming.
